examples.title: Thread Creation Using Pthreads
examples.description: Demonstrates basic thread creation using POSIX Pthreads library
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates One-to-One threading model using POSIX Pthreads. Each pthread_create() call creates both a user thread and maps it to a kernel thread. The pthread_join() waits for thread completion.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of threads created

examples.title: Thread Pool Implementation
examples.description: Demonstrates thread pooling concept using Python's concurrent.futures
examples.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
import time

def task(task_id):
    print(f"Task {task_id} started by thread")
    time.sleep(1)  # Simulating work
    print(f"Task {task_id} completed")
    return f"Result of task {task_id}"

def main():
    # Create a thread pool with 3 worker threads
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
        # Submit 6 tasks to the pool
        futures = []
        for i in range(6):
            future = executor.submit(task, i)
            futures.append(future)
        
        # Wait for all tasks to complete
        for future in futures:
            result = future.result()
            print(f"Got result: {result}")
    
    print("All tasks completed")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates thread pooling. Instead of creating 6 separate threads, we create a pool of 3 workers that process 6 tasks from a queue. This reduces thread creation overhead and demonstrates the thread pool concept used in many-to-M many models.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of tasks, bounded by pool size for parallelism
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum number of worker threads in the pool

examples.title: Thread Synchronization with Mutex
examples.description: Demonstrates thread synchronization using mutex to protect shared resource
examples.code:
SHARED VARIABLES:
    counter = 0
    mutex = MUTEX()

FUNCTION increment_counter(thread_id):
    // Lock the mutex before accessing shared resource
    mutex.lock()
    
    local_copy = counter
    local_copy = local_copy + 1
    counter = local_copy
    
    print("Thread " + thread_id + " incremented counter to " + counter)
    
    // Unlock the mutex after access
    mutex.unlock()

FUNCTION main():
    threads = []
    
    // Create 5 threads that all increment the counter
    FOR i FROM 1 TO 5:
        thread = CREATE_THREAD(increment_counter, i)
        threads.append(thread)
    
    // Wait for all threads
    FOR each thread IN threads:
        JOIN_THREAD(thread)
    
    PRINT("Final counter value: " + counter)
    // Without synchronization, final value might be less than 5
    // With synchronization, final value will be exactly 5
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates thread synchronization using a mutex (mutual exclusion lock). Without synchronization, multiple threads reading and writing to the shared counter simultaneously would cause a race condition, potentially losing updates. The mutex ensures only one thread can access the critical section at a time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of threads
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space for mutex, O(n) for thread storage

examples.title: Java Multithreading with Thread Class
examples.description: Demonstrates One-to-One threading model in Java
examples.explanation: This Java program demonstrates the One-to-One threading model. Each Thread.start() call creates a new thread at both user level (Java thread) and kernel level (JVM maps to kernel thread). The join() method waits for thread completion. Java's threading model uses native threads, providing true parallelism on multi-core systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for creating and running n threads
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for thread objects and their stacks
